attrs.alt: Conditional Access signal categories: user/group, application/action, location, sign-in risk, user risk, and device/client app conditions

content.text: Conditional Access conditions are the 'if' side of a CA policy. Each condition narrows which sign-ins the policy applies to. Conditions within a single policy are ANDed together: all specified conditions must match for the policy to fire. Unspecified conditions default to matching everything — a policy with no location condition applies from any location; a policy with no device condition applies to any device.

The six condition categories are: user and group identity, application and action, location, sign-in risk (from Identity Protection), user risk (from Identity Protection), and client app type and device platform. Understanding each category — what it detects, what its values mean, and when to use it — is essential for writing policies that target exactly the right sign-ins without over-blocking or under-protecting.

The users condition specifies who the policy applies to using include and exclude lists. Both lists support individual user OIDs, security group OIDs, directory role template IDs, and special values like 'All' or 'GuestsOrExternalUsers'.
Every user in the tenant including guests, unless excluded
B2B guest users, B2B direct connect users, and local guest accounts
All current and future members of the specified group
All users who currently hold the specified directory role
Policy does not apply to any user — effectively disabled

# Get directory role template IDs for use in CA policy role targeting
$roles = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryRoleTemplates'

# Find the template ID for specific roles
$roles.value | Where-Object { $_.displayName -in @(
    'Global Administrator',
    'Security Administrator',
    'Privileged Role Administrator',
    'User Administrator'
)} | Select-Object displayName, id

# Common role template IDs:
# Global Administrator:          62e90394-69f5-4237-9190-012177145e10
# Security Administrator:        194ae4cb-b126-40b2-bd5b-6091b380977d
# Privileged Role Administrator: e8611ab8-c189-46e8-94e1-60213ab1f814
# User Administrator:            fe930be7-5e62-47db-91af-98c3a49a38b1
# Exchange Administrator:        29232cdf-9323-42fd-ade2-1d097af3e4de

Targeting by role (includeRoles) is dynamic — the policy automatically applies to whoever holds the role at sign-in time, without needing to update a group when personnel change. This is the recommended approach for admin-focused CA policies.

The applications condition controls which apps the policy targets. 'All cloud apps' covers every app registered with Entra that uses OAuth2 or SAML. 'User actions' targets specific user behaviors in Entra itself — registering security information (MFA/SSPR) or registering/joining a device — rather than access to a specific app.
Every cloud app including Office 365, Azure management, and custom apps
All Office 365 apps as a group: Exchange, SharePoint, Teams, OneDrive
A specific enterprise app by its application ID (client ID)
Azure portal, Entra admin center, Intune portal, and other Microsoft admin UIs
Azure Service Management API (Azure portal + ARM) — well-known ID

Location conditions filter sign-ins by the IP address of the client. Named locations can be defined as IP ranges (CIDR blocks) or as country/region sets. A location marked as 'trusted' gets special treatment — it is the only type that satisfies the 'All trusted locations' filter, and policies can exclude it to target only untrusted networks.

# Create an IP-range named location (corporate network)
$ipLocation = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.ipNamedLocation'
    displayName   = 'Corporate HQ and VPN'
    isTrusted     = $true
    ipRanges      = @(
        @{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.iPv4CidrRange'; cidrAddress = '203.0.113.0/24' },
        @{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.iPv4CidrRange'; cidrAddress = '198.51.100.0/24' }
    )
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

$loc = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/namedLocations' \
  -Body $ipLocation -ContentType 'application/json'
Write-Host "Location ID: $($loc.id)"

# Create a country/region named location (allowed countries)
$countryLocation = @{
    '@odata.type'               = '#microsoft.graph.countryNamedLocation'
    displayName                  = 'Allowed countries'
    isTrusted                    = $false
    countriesAndRegions          = @('US', 'CA', 'GB', 'DE', 'AU')
    includeUnknownCountriesAndRegions = $false
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/namedLocations' \
  -Body $countryLocation -ContentType 'application/json'

Country-based filtering uses IP geolocation and is best-effort — VPNs and Tor exit nodes can make a sign-in appear to come from any country. Use it as a first-pass signal combined with risk conditions, not as a hard security boundary.
Sign-in Risk and User Risk Conditions

Risk conditions integrate Microsoft Entra ID Protection's machine learning signals into CA policy decisions. Sign-in risk reflects properties of a single authentication event (atypical travel, anonymous IP, malware-linked IP, impossible travel). User risk reflects the cumulative risk score of the account over time (leaked credentials in dark web, anomalous patterns).
Strong indicators of compromise: leaked credential match, sign-in from known attack infrastructure, impossible travel combined with suspicious app access
Confirmed or highly probable credential leak found in breach datasets
Multiple weaker indicators combined: unfamiliar sign-in properties, anonymous IP with atypical behavior
Some anomalous user activity detected; possibly compromised but not confirmed
Single weak indicator: slightly unfamiliar sign-in properties, atypical token
Minor anomaly detected; low confidence of compromise
No anomalies detected in this sign-in
No anomalies detected for this user account

Device filters allow CA policies to target or exclude specific devices based on Entra device attributes. This is more granular than the built-in grant controls (compliant device, hybrid-joined device) — filters can match specific device properties like trust type, operating system version, or a custom extension attribute set by Intune.

The clientAppTypes condition distinguishes between how the client is authenticating. This is critical for legacy authentication blocking — older protocols like NTLM, Basic Auth, and IMAP/POP do not support MFA challenges, so they must be blocked entirely rather than redirected to MFA.
Web browser OAuth2 flows (Chrome, Edge, Safari, Firefox)
Modern native apps using MSAL, ADAL, or OAuth2 device code flow
Exchange ActiveSync (email clients using EAS protocol, e.g., iOS Mail app configured with Exchange)
Only if app supports modern auth
All other protocols: NTLM, Basic Auth, IMAP, POP, SMTP relay, legacy MAPI

Authentication contexts let individual applications or resources request a specific level of assurance beyond what the initial sign-in provided. A resource registers an authentication context class reference (e.g., 'c1'), then asks Entra ID to evaluate a CA policy scoped to that context when a user accesses a protected operation. This enables step-up MFA without forcing the entire session to reauthenticate.
Custom step-up flows (admin portal, financial app)
SharePoint sites tagged with label → triggers auth context

Authentication contexts are commonly used for SharePoint sensitivity label enforcement, Azure PIM activation gates, and custom B2E portals that host a mix of low-sensitivity and high-sensitivity pages in the same application.

Continuous Access Evaluation reduces the window between a security event (password change, session revoke, IP location change) and CA policy enforcement. Instead of waiting up to one hour for a standard access token to expire, supported services receive real-time push notifications from Entra ID and immediately revoke the session.
Token revoked if policy enforces location

# CAE is enabled by default for supported apps (Exchange, Teams, SharePoint)
# To verify CAE token claims in a received token:

# Decode an access token to check CAE claim
$token = 'eyJ0...'  # access token from auth flow
$payload = $token.Split('.')[1]
# Pad to multiple of 4 for Base64
$padded = $payload.PadRight(($payload.Length + 3) / 4 * 4, '=')
$decoded = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String($padded))
$claims = $decoded | ConvertFrom-Json

# CAE-capable tokens contain xms_cc claim
# $claims.xms_cc.values should include 'cp1'
Write-Host 'CAE capable:' ($claims.xms_cc.values -contains 'cp1')

# In CA policy, enforce CAE strict enforcement for critical apps
# (prevents token reuse after password change, account disable, or location change)
Update-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -ConditionalAccessPolicyId '<policy-id>' \
    -SessionControls @{
        ContinuousAccessEvaluation = @{ Mode = 'strictLocation' }
    }

CAE strict-location mode is the most aggressive enforcement setting — it revokes tokens the moment the user's IP leaves a CA-trusted location, rather than waiting for token expiry or the next sign-in.
Insider Risk Integration (Microsoft Purview Adaptive Protection)

When Microsoft Purview Insider Risk Management is enabled and Adaptive Protection is turned on, Purview streams a per-user risk level signal into CA. Policies can then target 'insiderRiskLevels' as a condition, enabling automated access restriction that scales with the severity of insider risk signals — without manual intervention.
Low-severity policy match (e.g., occasional data exfil attempt)
Multiple policy matches or medium severity activity
Block all cloud app access, restrict sessions
Named Location Audit and Trusted Network Management

Named locations drift over time as offices move, VPN gateways change, or country policies are updated. Regularly audit all named locations to verify that IP ranges reflect actual corporate egress points, that trusted flags are not over-applied (every trusted range reduces friction for all MFA CA policies), and that no stale or overly broad CIDR blocks remain.
Use /24 or narrower CIDR ranges — overly broad ranges (e.g., /8) can inadvertently include ISP addresses shared with attackers.
isTrusted = true counts as a 'compliant network' exclusion in CA policies and reduces MFA prompts — apply it only to known corporate egress IPs.
includeUnknownCountriesAndRegions = true in country-block policies catches satellite and anonymizing proxies not mapped to any country code.
Named locations are shared across all CA policies — renaming or deleting one affects every policy that references it; always verify policy references before changes.
CA conditions are ANDed within a policy — all specified conditions must match the sign-in for the policy to apply. Omitted conditions match everything.
User conditions support individual users, groups, directory roles (using template IDs), and guest/external user categories. Targeting by role is dynamic and tracks role membership automatically.

Application conditions target specific app client IDs or the All / Office365 / MicrosoftAdminPortals special values. User action conditions target registration flows (MFA setup, device registration) rather than app access.
Location conditions filter by named IP ranges or country/region. Mark corporate IP ranges as trusted to enable 'exclude trusted locations' patterns in other policies.
Risk conditions (sign-in risk, user risk) require Entra ID P2 and integrate Identity Protection ML signals in real time. Use block for High risk and require MFA for Medium risk.

Client app type conditions distinguish modern (browser, MSAL) from legacy (Basic Auth, EAS, other) clients. Blocking the 'exchangeActiveSync' and 'other' types eliminates legacy auth vectors that cannot satisfy MFA.
